nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs over rubble and sand bottoms, from shallow silty estuaries and inlets out to outer reef slopes; typically 1–10 m depth (recorded down to about 50 m). Adults occupy burrows (which they do not generally excavate themselves) and hover just above the substrate feeding on current-borne zooplankton, usually in monogamous pairs or small groups.

decorPreferences: open sand bed at least 5 cm deep for burrowing or hiding
loose coral rubble piled to form hiding crevices
live rock with small caves and overhangs
open swimming space above the substrate

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on current-borne zooplankton hovering near its burrow. In captivity generally accepts mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, copepods, finely chopped krill or table shrimp, and frozen carnivore preparations. Multiple small feedings per day (commonly suggested 2–3 or more) are generally advised, particularly during acclimation when individuals tend to be shy.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2673x2005. Sharp in-situ hover shot showing the pearly silver-blue body, green-tinged head and bright green eye, with more of the group behind it.
Debra Baker (via GBIF/iNaturalist); 1536x2048. Group of several individuals gathered around a burrow, showing the fine red lateral line and typical paired/social behaviour; slightly soft.
Izuzuki; 640x480. In-situ pair against dark background; main fish crisply lit showing pearly blue-green flanks and green eye. Lower resolution, kept as variety extra.

summary: The Green Silk Gudgeon Dartfish is a peaceful, reef-safe Indo-Pacific dartfish generally considered beginner-friendly. It tends to hover just above the sand bed near burrows it shares with a mate and is known to be a jumper, so a tightly sealed lid is generally advised.
careNotes:
The Green Silk Gudgeon Dartfish is generally advised to be kept in an established reef aquarium or fish-only-with-live-rock system of at least 150 liters. A sandy substrate at least 5 cm deep is commonly recommended, along with loose coral rubble arranged to form hiding crevices and live rock with small caves or overhangs. Although this species has been reported to occupy burrows in the wild, it generally does not excavate them itself, so providing ready-made hiding spaces is important for helping the fish settle in.

New arrivals tend to be quite shy. Offering multiple small feedings per day — two to three or more — and keeping lighting subdued during the first few days have been reported to help draw the fish out and encourage it to feed. Once established, it generally accepts mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, copepods, finely chopped krill or table shrimp, and frozen carnivore preparations.

The Green Silk Gudgeon Dartfish is widely regarded as a jumper, and a tight-fitting lid with no gaps is generally considered essential for keeping it safely in the aquarium.

This species is generally considered peaceful and docile toward all tankmates, including others of its own kind. It is commonly reported to do best when kept singly or as a bonded male-female pair; in the wild, adults are commonly observed forming monogamous pairs that share a burrow. Overtly aggressive or predatory tankmates — including groupers, dottybacks, triggerfish, aggressive angelfishes, and lionfish — are best avoided, as these tend to stress or harass this mild-mannered fish.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been well documented in commonly available hobbyist literature. In the wild, adults are commonly reported to form monogamous pairs sharing a burrow, but specific aquarium spawning protocols are not established.
